questions.prompt: Which set of languages does Glean ship typed Developer Platform clients for?
options: Python, TypeScript, Java, Go
Python and JavaScript only
Ruby, PHP, C#, Rust
There is no SDK; you must use raw HTTP
questions.explanation: Glean provides typed clients for Python, TypeScript, Java, and Go over the same platform APIs.

questions.prompt: Why does a user-scoped token return different results for two different users running the same query?
options: It caches per user at random
It carries the caller's identity, so Glean filters by that user's permissions
It only searches the user's own documents
It sorts results differently per user
questions.explanation: A user-scoped token impersonates one user, so Glean enforces that user's permissions and returns only what they may see.

questions.prompt: In a Chat API response, what do the citations tell you?
options: How fast the answer was generated
The documents the answer is grounded in — your basis for trusting it
The user's recent searches
Which model produced the answer
questions.explanation: Citations are the grounding sources. No citations means the answer is ungrounded and should not be trusted as fact.

questions.prompt: You need to make 10,000 support tickets searchable in Glean. Which API do you use?
options: The Client API
The Indexing API
The Web SDK
The Search API
questions.explanation: Writing content into the graph is the Indexing API's job; the Client API only reads what is already indexed.

questions.prompt: When pushing documents through the Indexing API, why must you include permission metadata?
options: It speeds up indexing
It is optional styling for the results page
Glean enforces only the permissions you push, so without ACLs the content is not access-controlled
It sets the document's display title
questions.explanation: Permissions ship with the document; Glean can only filter by ACLs you actually push, so omitting them leaves the content ungoverned.
